data.title: Public Storage: You Can Lock 6% From The Preferreds 'Long Term'
data.description:
Public Storage (PSA) is one of the largest REITs in the world. The company manages over 3,400 properties with more than 250 million square feet of leasable space and has a well-established reputation as a market leader in the self-storage sector. Its preferred stocks offer a yield of around 6% - a yield that can easily be "held forever" by a long-term investor seeking a combination of security and steady cash flow.

Company Basic Information

  • Total Balance Sheet Assets (Q3): approximately $ 20 billion
  • Current market capitalization: close to $48 billion
  • Portfolio: around 3491 properties, 254 million rentable square feet
  • Preferred stocks: ~ $4.35 billion
  • Total equity: approximately $9.4 billion
  • Debt: Close to $ 10 billion
  • Credit rating: Moody's: "A2" / S&P: "A"

Financial metrics

  • Asset Yield ((total revenue - operating expenses + depreciation and amortization) / total assets): 16.8%
  • Asset Coverage ratio (total assets/debt): 200%
  • Operating expenses/Total revenue (excluding depreciation and amortization): 29.5%
  • Interest expenses/Debt: 2.94%

We also calculate market-adjusted asset yield to be more accurate. Therefore, we will need a price-to-book value, which is, at the time of writing, 9.7. We will subtract total equity from total assets and add the new one, multiplied by P/B. From here, we can calculate the market-adjusted yield and the market-adjusted asset coverage ratio.

  • Market Adjusted Asset Yield: 3.31%
  • Market Adjusted Asset Coverage ratio: 670% (extremely high ratio, among the highest in the sector)
  • Historical performance:

  • PSA's 5-year total return on NAV is close to 7.56%, and the 10-year total return is around 5.06%.
  • Expected FFO yield: 6.18%

Preferred stocks

Above are shown PSA's preferred stocks. All have current yields around 6%, and all are trading below par. Moody's rates them with a credit rating of "A3", and S&P rates them with a credit rating of "BBB+" - so the stocks fall in the "investment-grade" category. Since preferred shares share many similarities, we will show you 2 in more detail-one with the highest current yield (PSA.PR.K) and the other (PSA.PR.P) with the least-narrowed spread relative to the Treasury yield (see below for the spreads of the other PSA stocks).

PSA.PR.K pays cumulative distributions of 4.75% annual amount, or $1.19 per annum, in the form of $0.296785 quarterly. PSA issued 8 million shares from the stock on 12/11/2019, each with a par value of $25, or gross proceeds of 200 million. The call date of the stock has passed, so it can be called anytime. The spread to the 20-year treasury yield at its IPO was 2.54%, and the spread now is close to 1.42%. The current yield of the stock is close to 6.08%. Below, you can compare the different yields against the different potential stock prices of PSA.PR.K:

A total of 21 million shares of PSA.PR.P were issued on 6/7/2021, each with a par value of $25, or gross proceeds of $525 million. At the IPO date, spreads over the treasury yield averaged about 1.87%. Currently, this spread is about 1.36%. The stock pays a cumulative distribution of 4% per annum, or $1 annual amount, distributed quarterly at $0.25 per share. The call date of the stock is on 06/26/2026, with approximately half a year to call. Its current yield is around 6.02%. You can see in the chart different prices vs. different yields to worst of PSA.PR.P:

OTC bonds

Above are shown PSA's over-the-counter bonds. All are rated with a credit rating "A2" by Moody's. The highest-yielding bond is close to a 5.5% yield to maturity.

If we look at the whole REIT sector, the highest yield to maturity is around 5.7%. When comparing PSA preferred shares to OTC bonds, it is clear that preferreds offer a higher yield (even slightly higher), with comparable credit risk - both categories are investment grade. In addition, preferred shares have preferential tax treatment for some investors, and the after-tax yield spread might be even more tempting.

Credibility

Even if there is already a credit rating, we like to dig into the details and double-check everything, so let's look at the credit rating according to Moody's methodology:

  1. Gross assets: Aa3. PSA has close to $20 bln in gross assets (however, if we adjust this figure by incorporating market capitalization, both the adjusted asset base and the implied credit rating would be higher).
  2. Unencumbered assets: A2. Most of the assets are unencumbered.
  3. Secured debt: PSA has almost no secured debt, so the rating here is Aaa.
  4. Total debt + preferred stock as a percentage of gross assets: Baa3. The company's total debt and preferred stock are close to 45% of its total assets.
  5. Net debt to EBITDA: Baa1. Using total debt and an EBITDA of $3.37 billion, the resulting ratio is approximately 4.17.
  6. Fixed charge coverage: A1. EBITDA covers interest and preferred stock dividends at a rate of nearly 6.8 times.
  7. Market positioning and asset quality, and operating environment: A2.
  8. Liquidity and access to capital: A1. The company's operating expenses (excluding depreciation and amortization) represent about 29.5% of revenue. Its EBITDA comfortably covers interest and preferred share dividends nearly 7 times. PSA is among the companies able to finance their preferred stocks at the lowest rates on the exchange. We remember how they were among the first to break the 4% nominal yield barrier back in 2021.

Here's what PSA's overall credit ratings look like: A2. Adjusting with the market cap overall balance sheet score, we would get an A1 credit rating.

Our adjusted credit score is even higher compared to the actual credit rating of PSA. It just does not get much safer in terms of credit risk in the REIT space. So it all comes to duration risk. PSA preferred stocks are one of the perfect candidates for income, in our opinion, especially if one believes we have seen the bottom in the bond market.

Conclusion

PSA continues to be a symbol of stability in the REIT sector. With a high credit rating, excellent liquidity, and a balanced capital structure, the company offers investors a rare combination of low risk and attractive returns. The preferred shares, with a current yield of around 6%, stand out as an extremely suitable vehicle for long-term investors seeking reliable income with the potential for moderate capital gains.

data.title: Eagle Point Income: Why I Own A Combination Of The Common And Preferred Shares
data.description:
Introduction

A lot of income investors appear to be surprised to see the distributions of BDCs and CLO-focused vehicles come under pressure. That shouldn’t come as a surprise, as the majority of the investments in those vehicles are floating rate based yet it's surprising how many investors are unhappy with distribution cuts even though that should have been widely expected given the evolution of the interest rates on the financial markets.

I have been following Eagle Point Credit (EIC) I own the common shares, and I own some of the preferred shares, so I'm keeping tabs on its financial performance.

The NII remains strong

I was of course curious to see the net investment income realized by Eagle Point. As the image below shows, the company reported total investment income of approximately $16.2M during the third quarter of the year. While that’s an increase compared to the first two quarters of the year, the operating expenses increased as well. But despite that, the net investment income for the quarter came in at $10.1M.

It's important to know that because the preferred shares issued by Eagle Point Income are term preferred shares with a mandatory redemption date, the preferred dividends are actually seen as interest expenses, and are thus included in the operating expenses. In fact, as the income statement above shows, in excess of half of the operating expenses are related to interest expenses (and preferred dividends). And as the balance sheet below shows, there are no other financial liabilities on the balance sheet, which means there are no real "interest payments" that are senior to preferred dividend payments.

That’s also important to determine the attractiveness of the preferred stock. Eagle Pint Income ended the quarter with almost $564M in assets (including $55M in cash and interest receivable), with just $208M in liabilities. This means that even if the portfolio securities would see a 65% drop in its value, the preferred shareholders would still be made whole (assuming the $14.6M in interest receivable effectively gets paid out).

That’s important as Eagle Point Income also has to adhere to the 200% rule: The total amount of net assets has to exceed the total value of the preferred stock (and debt) by a factor of 2. As you can see below, at the end of September, EIC handsomely met this requirement as the total amount of preferred capital outstanding was just $192.4M vs. $547M in net assets. This indeed represented an asset coverage ratio of 285%, so well above the 200% mandatory minimum.

I sometimes get the question, "what happens if EIC risks falling below that 200% threshold?" The answer to that is pretty simple: The company can retire preferred stock to reduce the total amount outstanding, or EIC could just print more common shares to add assets by issuing junior equity. Using the proceeds from asset sales to retire preferred stock also is a possibility, but the pricing of the securities could not be optimal should markets get really volatile.

I now own two series of the preferred shares

I have a long position in the Series A and Series C preferred shares issued by Eagle Point Income, and the Series B (EICB) is the sole security issued by EIC that I currently do not own. However, as the mandatory call date for the Series A is coming closer (in just over 11 months from now), I’m keeping an eye on the Series B to determine if they could be a valid replacement.

The Series B preferred stock offers a preferred dividend of 7.75% or $1.9375 per year, payable in monthly tranches. While this series is callable right now, there also is a mandatory redemption date for these securities; EIC has to redeem the Series B by July 31 2028 the latest. As such, we should look at this from a yield to maturity perspective and a duration of just under 2.7 years from now.

As you can see above, EICB is currently trading at $25.02, resulting in a yield to maturity of just over 7.7%. This represents a mark-up of 410 bps versus the 3 year US Treasury rate. While preferred equity of course still is "just equity," the lack of more senior preferred or debt securities is a bonus.

Investment thesis

I like the risk/reward ratio of the preferred shares, as the dividend coverage ratio is excellent, while the asset coverage ratio came in at almost 300% (substantially higher than the required 200% minimum). I currently own EICA and EICC to mix short-term with mid-term income visibility, but I would, of course, also have a closer look at EICB depending on the share price and the remaining duration of the security.

I also have a long position in the common stock issued by EIC, and I will likely add to my position in the next few days. I think all securities issued by Eagle Point Income are a "buy" right now, and the choice between EICA, EICB, and EICC is mainly a question of the desired duration.

data.title: 3 Observations On Why I'm Not Afraid Of A Crash
data.description:
Air Bubble Coming Out Of Speculative Plays

A volatile week ended with some respite for investors, but not necessarily relief yet, even as dip buyers fended off the sellers who have gained the upper hand for the past three weeks. The selling intensity in speculative stocks has persisted, with southbound traffic dominating the recent sentiments, as market rotation themes were clearly in the mind of investors.

Small caps are also coming under scrutiny, with the Russell 2000 (RTY) (IWM) underperforming their large cap peers since the peak in mid-October. Therefore, if there's something for us to learn from the recent roller coaster ride, I believe it's a very timely reminder that momentum swings aren't easily predictable and could also very quickly reverse to the downside, as the market faces up to reality at this stage of the advance.

What reality am I talking about? Well, investors who bought into the FOMO peaks in October could be adjudged to have been ignorant of underlying risks that have manifested for some time in speculative assets. In addition, concerns regarding stretched valuations have also persisted for some time, but the market continues to grind higher, baffling these bears. I believe it demonstrates that, while the valuation narrative is front and center right now, I do not think they are sufficient per se, in leading to a market correction or bear market unless investors decide to price in earnings multiples compression risks due to an increasingly likely recession.

Perhaps, that could explain why the rally from the April lows have still maintained a favorable uptrend for the tech-led advance, even as certain sectors such as energy (XLE) and healthcare (XLV) stumbled for traction previously. However, I have also put forth my views in a recent update that, we are seeing signs of a market rotation afoot, benefiting these erstwhile ignored setups, coinciding with the selloff in speculative stocks.

Recession Not The Base Case

Despite that, the fact is that the pullback hasn't devolved into a more worrying correction yet, with the S&P 500 just 4% below its recent highs. So, calling it a market crash (other than what was observed in the speculative corners) isn't apt, not even close. And, the other fact is that Wall Street's EPS estimates have actually been upgraded through the next 12 months, flummoxing investors who thought the recent volatility doesn't quite seem to align with improved fundamentals.

However, for those investors willing to move beyond the obvious headlines in the media clamoring (solely or mostly) about heightened valuations, they might find useful insights to decode the recent market behavior. They might also glean more helpful indicators to better comprehend the current market reality, while attempting to demystify the rhetoric from their investment process.

First up, recession risks. For now, the base case of a recession is still relatively low. However, that doesn't necessarily indicate that economists are right on this count, just like how they got it wrong back in 2022, as the most anticipated recession in recent times failed to arrive. Will it arrive in 2026, catching the economics profession off-guard yet again? Strategist and economist Ed Yardeni has expounded on this matter in depth over the years. And in these instances, he has also given us helpful pointers to contemplate, when thinking about whether the narrative in the financial media holds enough weight to justify their optimism/skepticism. And for that of a recession, he underscores certain preconditions:

There are three ways that recessions typically are triggered: 1) a monetary-tightening-induced financial crisis turns into a credit crunch; 2) a geopolitical crisis causes oil prices to soar; or 3) speculative asset bubbles burst. All three have caused recessions in the past. - Ed Yardeni on The Causes of Recessions & Bear Markets, a Recap

A cursory glance will have informed us that a monetary tightening led crisis isn't likely? It's unlike that in 2022, where we dealt with inflation rates that were not seen in recent years. While we can argue whether we might have a rate cut in December 2025, the consensus remains favorable of just below three 25 bps cuts in the next 12 months. Hence, I think we can generally put 1) in the KIV list for now.

How about 2) "geopolitical crisis causes oil prices to soar"? You don't need special skills in recognizing the downtrend bias in crude oil prices (CL1:COM) (CO1:COM) right now to know that the top surfaced in 2022. Since then, oil prices have not mustered enough conviction to continue its preceding uptrend.

And the recent reports emphasizing the potential for a "record oil surplus" in 2026 will continue to mount more pressure on energy bulls in the foreseeable future. While the sporadic geopolitical dials may lift sentiments on oil prices from time to time, it's somewhat unrealistic for us to think that these are sufficient to spur a recession over the next year.

So, that brings us to No.3), which marks an implosion of a speculative asset bubble. I believe I've addressed these in greater depth in my previous writeups, expounding on why bubble fears are more pronounced than the bubble itself right now. While the speculative companies with no or little cash flow are being hammered right now, the Magnificent Seven leading the AI buildout right now are still minting resilient cash flow, while also providing confidence to the public debt market to bolster their external financing needs.

AI Jobs Recession Overhyped?

Hence, we turn our attention to other segments of the economy to assess whether fears of a so-called K-shaped economy could pervade into elevating stress sensors monitoring the health of the consumer.

On this aspect, there are clues that consumers are less optimistic about the overall economy than what has been forecasted by economists. These clearly tie in with recent surveys illuminating the gloomy state of consumer confidence. Therefore, even as the economy is still expected to hum along relatively well, consumers are surprisingly less sanguine than what these official channels purvey.

Moving toward the next year, the disconnect isn't expected to close the gap. Media reports highlighting Verizon's expected 15K jobs cuts (VZ) should worsen fears about jobs displacement risks linked to AI, even though it's not immediately clear right now whether they truly reflect the actual dynamics on the ground. I believe that efficiency-driven reductions shouldn't be construed negatively, unless a wide array of job cuts due to AI are promulgated across the economy.

Have we seen that kind of "AI apocalyse" yet? Not really, right? And the reticence from the recent Fed narrative suggests that the balancing act between employment and inflation is skewing in favor of curbing inflationary worries next month, than mitigating the impact from a feared widespread jobs market doom.

I think it's way too nascent for us to broadcast thesis suggesting that AI could cause massive unemployment right now, as AI-led productivity boosts to current jobs may not have been fully captured currently. With surging AI experimentation and adoption, has it corresponded with a steep decline in employment just yet? I don't think so, right? So it seems like companies clearly have flexibility in AI deployment, and potentially view them as supportive in boosting productivity with humans behind the wheel. In other words, AI augmentation, not replacement.

We May Have Entered Enthusiastic Stage. What's Next?

So, I believe what has arguably occurred could be very much aligned with the typical sentiments driven market cycle that saw the air coming out of the speculative bubble. We don't need to go back to the 1999/2000 dot com bust to remind ourselves that such selling fervor could percolate into the broader market.

Back in the heights of the pandemic bubble in 2021/22, investors should recall how tech and growth stocks topped in late 2021 (peak enthusiasm), before the broader market peaked about a couple of months later in January 2022, which then culminated in a bear market (without a recession, mind you). It then bottomed in late 2022 as the market hit peak discouragement. So, paying close to the sentiments cycle could unveil helpful insights into parsing the current market conditions.

Take a glance at the current sentiments cycle composite. We have undoubtedly entered the enthusiasm stage, setting the wheels in motion for an eventual bull market peak scenario to play out. Yet, this chart also shows that such a turn has yet to be ascertained.

That means, the enthusiastic stage could persist for an even longer time, notwithstanding the fears of an imminent bubble implosion inundating us in the financial media right now. Hence, the reality is clear. Sentiments suggest we have the preconditions necessary for a bull market top, but it has yet to indicate a turn in the cycle. Momentum remains a formidable driving force, and one that unprofitable short sellers would have learned about in the past when they timed their bets incorrectly against high momentum plays, betting inaccurately on their reversals.

Greed & Fear Indicators Aren't At FOMO Levels Now

Next, a quick glance on whether shorter term sentiment indicators show a distinct FOMO top that could highlight that a turn in the sentiments cycle is brewing? Interestingly, if we consider a medium-term duration moving average of the Fear & Greed Model, it hit a peak in July and has been falling ever since. It shows that the deep worries about an AI bubble may have manifested, but clearly not enough to bring down the overall market, as it scaled new highs in October.

Pessimistic concerns continue in earnest through the current weak lows, suggesting that the current top doesn't quite have corroborating FOMO signals from near term sentiment indicators showing that a ultimate bull market top is inevitable.

I hope that I've afforded you some useful nuggets, showing that the market isn't as weak as some in the media may have anticipated. Even the sentiment cycles (short and long term) don't show clear signals of a bull market peak yet.

Therefore, even if we were to fall further into a correction, it could very well be an opportunity to load up on high quality plays in this stage of the market cycle, as we ride the prevailing trend to a subsequent high. With recessionary signals and preconditions not assessed as the base case, I don't expect a broad collapse in valuation multiples to occur. That should prod us to maintain our optimistic perspective, and leaning in to buy those pullbacks as they occur from time to time.

data.title: AI Bubble? Alphabet's New $40B Bet Only Raises The Stakes
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Although I recently covered Alphabet’s (NASDAQ:GOOG) resilient cloud strategy, I feel compelled to reiterate my stance on the company for two reasons that have become even more material in light of recent events over the past week.

First, the narrative about the AI bubble potentially bursting has snowballed into a spaghetti ball of messy conclusions that take away the significance of growth ramps that AI beneficiaries like Alphabet have built in the past 24-36 months. I will address this briefly towards the end of my thesis for Alphabet, but not before I unravel Alphabet’s recent $40B bet.

Second, Alphabet announced yesterday that it will be investing $40B into developing new data centers in Texas, which hopefully puts the attention back on management’s relentless efforts to march ahead with its goals of dominating its AI ambitions in the cloud market.

These investments by Alphabet announced yesterday signal one thing—Main Street can rage about the AI bubble, but for management it’s still business as usual with revenue growth and free cash margins that still need to be delivered.

I am raising my PT and reiterating my Buy rating on Alphabet’s shares.

Even Berkshire Hathway Agrees With Alphabet’s AI Cloud Strategy

Earlier this month when I wrote about Alphabet, I noted how the company’s management had relentlessly focused on AI to reorient the company towards a position of strength, with Google Cloud growing at an extremely impressive pace of a +30% growth rate while the Search business was continuing to inflect as well.

I also explained how Alphabet was picking up many pages from the strategy book of Nvidia’s hypergrowth story to build solid growth ramps for its TPU (tensor processing units) business and start offering them independently versus the prior approach of offering TPUs via mandatory GCP (Google Cloud Platform) subscriptions.

This strategy seemed to be working very well for GCP, which was growing at a +30% rate. What I did not show in the previous article was GCP’s market share, which now commands ~13% of the cloud market, growing from 10% four years ago, making it one of the fastest-growing cloud players.

Growth has only accelerated so far this year for GCP, and I believe Alphabet’s recent $40B bet will only be another enabler to accelerate GCP growth even further. What is not clear from the announcement is whether Alphabet is making this data center investment for an existing client or to rapidly build capacity to attract new clients. The $40B investment does signal Google’s intention to develop $40B worth of data centers in Texas following a roadmap that ensures delivery of the data center through CY27. Either way, the CY27 timeline is beneficial to Google Cloud (and Alphabet) because it would imply faster recognition of cloud backlog it may have scored from recent wins like the new Anthropic deal worth “tens of billions of dollars” or help GCP attract more customer/program wins like the Anthropic deal or like the $10B deal between GCP and Meta Platforms (META).

What’s even better is the level of operational efficiency that management is demonstrating while rapidly growing the GCP business portfolio. Since alarm bells were ringing after ChatGPT’s global launch and the “Code Red” that was issued at the time, GCP has grown to a +30% growth business operating at 20% operating margins, delivering ~272 bp of margin expansion per quarter since Q4 22.

This is a rare combination of scale, growth, and margin expansion, which upheld my belief in the company’s vision for cloud and AI, and I now see that even Berkshire Hathaway has picked up a stake in Alphabet, making it their 11th largest holding.

Quick Note About The AI Bubble & Alphabet’s Road Ahead

The debate about the AI bubble bursting is raging across stock markets and is probably diluting the significant opportunity that lies ahead for investors. Despite the fears, companies like Alphabet and Anthropic have continued to strike deals, implying it's business as usual for tech pioneers. While Anthropic has partnered with Fluidstack, another partner of Alphabet, to build out $50B worth of data centers, Alphabet itself is investing $40B into Texas to build out data centers, just like I mentioned earlier.

One of the arguments for the AI bubble is that the investments that hyperscalers are making “puts their free cash flow generation at real risk,” per this Goldman Sachs report. But if investors peek under the hood, they will notice that Alphabet continues to generate free cash that is higher than its peer group’s free cash growth. I suspect this metric is definitely another area of operational efficiency that has piqued the interest of the team at Berkshire Hathaway.

Also, at a macro level, certain economists believe that “the economy is not weakening but strengthening.” Combine that viewpoint with the interest rate outlook that the Fed funds rate is expected to decline by at least 25-50 bp over the next 12 months, and investors will find themselves with enough bullish reasons to get back in the market again once the dust from the current pullback settles.

Upside In Alphabet’s Shares Rise

When I wrote my thesis for Alphabet earlier this month, I explained my rationale behind finding it appropriate to value the company’s shares at ~8.3x for a 13-14% expected revenue growth rate in CY26.

In short, my argument was that between CY22 and CY23, Alphabet grew its revenues by a CAGR of ~9% versus the 4.6-4.8x forward revenue multiples it traded at during those years.

Since my November coverage, I noticed that consensus estimates for Alphabet’s revenues have ironically been upgraded higher, with analysts now projecting ~14.2% growth in CY26. However, markets have overlooked the upgraded revenue growth projections for Alphabet because of inherent fears about the AI bubble bursting. This has pushed down Alphabet’s forward revenue multiple to ~7.2x CY26 revenues.

This makes the opportunity in Alphabet’s shares even more enticing since I believe the upgraded revenue growth projections should imply a valuation of ~8.6x, signaling upside of 19-20% from current levels or a PT of $332 per share.

Note: All references to revenue multiples are based on EV/forward revenues.

Risks To My Thesis

I do not expect any immediate deterioration in the AI investment landscape and the ecosystem so far based on the data points I see. I stay invested in Alphabet’s shares and continue to hold my shares for other AI beneficiaries because I believe end consumer demand is still benefitting AI players and AI infrastructure developers like Alphabet. Should the demand dry up, it will impact Alphabet’s outlook. That remains a risk that is not central to my thesis yet but is a factor I monitor in addition to my thesis.

Takeaway

I continue to be impressed by Alphabet’s relentless efforts in building on their growth and dominance in the AI landscape. Alphabet’s all-round efforts to be a full-stack AI company that not only builds its own frontier models but also develops the underlying infrastructure to support these models, while building commercial relationships at the same time, are all material reasons to buy Alphabet’s shares. The $40B investment to develop and deliver data centers by 2027 signals an aggressive growth ramp towards these goals.

I reiterate my Buy rating on Alphabet and am upgrading my PT.

data.title: Nvidia, Walmart Headline Earnings Next Week While U.S. Economic Data Returns
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street's major indexes were significantly lower on Thursday as heightened market sentiment due to the end of the U.S. government shutdown dissipated, and focus was turned to the economy. The U.S. House of Representatives on Wednesday passed the stopgap spending bill that will end the longest U.S. government shutdown in history, which lasted 43 days. With it, President Donald Trump signed into law a federal spending package. With the shutdown now over for the time being, investors are hoping that delayed economic reports will soon start to come. However, two major economic reports on jobs and inflation for October may never be released even as the government shutdown ends, according to White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt.

With the U.S. government shutdown now over, investors will keep an eye out for important economic reports. The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics confirmed that the delayed September Nonfarm Payrolls report will be published on Thursday. Other indicators on the docket are the NY Empire State Manufacturing Index, due to be released on Monday, and the ADP Employment Change, Industrial Production, and NAHB Housing Market Index on Tuesday. Wednesday will see the release of MBA Mortgage Applications, Building Permits, Atlanta Fed GDP, and FOMC Meeting Minutes, while Thursday will include the Philadelphia Fed Manufacturing Index and Existing Home Sales. Friday will include reports on S&P Global Manufacturing PMI and Michigan Consumer Sentiment.

XPeng (XPEV), Home Depot (HD), Medtronic (MDT), Baidu (BIDU), NVIDIA (NVDA), Palo Alto Networks (PANW), Lowe’s (LOW), Target (TGT), Walmart (WMT), and Intuit (INTU) will report earnings next week.

___________________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Monday: XPeng, Brady (BRC), Magic (MGIC), LifeMD (LFMD). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday: Home Depot, Medtronic, Baidu, Futu (FUTU), Amer Sports (AS). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday: NVIDIA, Palo Alto Networks (PANW), Lowe’s, and Target. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday: Walmart, Intuit, Jacobs Engineering (J). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Friday: MINISO (MNSO), Inventiva (IVA). See the full earnings calendar.

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend date coming next week include Apple (AAPL), American Electric Power (AEP), Carlyle (CG), Arrow Financial (AROW), Pool (POOL), Diamondback Energy (FANG), Zions Bancorporation (ZION), Honeywell (HON), and Starbucks (SBUX).

NVIDIA Earnings Preview: AI behemoth Nvidia is set to report third-quarter earnings next week, with analysts expecting the company to report earnings per share of $1.25 on revenue of $54.87B. According to several analysts, Nvidia is poised for significant growth ahead of its Q3 ’26 earnings, supported by robust AI infrastructure demand and a strong industry position. Hyperscalers like Microsoft, Alphabet, and Amazon are driving record capital outlays for compute capacity, reinforcing NVDA’s durable growth trajectory despite some competition from custom chips. NVDA’s dominance in both the U.S. and China is bolstered by licensing wins and technical challenges faced by Chinese competitors, supporting optimistic revenue forecasts for eFY27.

Walmart Earnings Preview: Retail giant Walmart is scheduled to report third-quarter earnings next week and is expected to report earnings per share of $0.60 on revenue of $175.14B. Several analysts have pointed out that Walmart remains resilient amid inflation and competition, supported by prudent inventory management, a diversified business model, and robust liquidity. WMT's low-cost, high-volume strategy, Walmart+ membership, and advertising growth drive customer retention and margin protection despite Amazon's dominance.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Synchrony Financial: Credit Continues To Defy Fears (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Synchrony Financial (SYF) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining 13%. Synchrony has weathered a volatile macro environment well as consumer credit has held up rather well. That said, there are some fears of a weakening labor market and signs of stress in some subprime lending classes (particularly auto), and SYF shares have been largely flat for about four months as a result. I last covered shares of Synchrony in July when I reiterated the stock as a hold, given valuation, and since then, shares are up 4%, which has lagged the market’s 7% gain and is consistent with my rating. With updated financials and a revised macro outlook, now is a good time to revisit SYF.

Q3 was strong as delinquencies remain muted

Looking first at its most recent results, in the company’s third quarter, reported back on October 15th, Synchrony smashed estimates, earning $2.86, which was $0.64 ahead of consensus. The company continues to report stabilization in credit quality, enabling some reserve releases and helping to defy fears of a credit rollover. Still, while reserves are coming down, they are above “normal” levels, reflecting an uncertain macroeconomic environment. This excess reserving also provides a margin of safety should economic activity deteriorate somewhat.

Encouragingly, we are actually seeing the opposite. Purchase volumes were up 2% to $46 billion. This was an improvement from -2% to Q2, which suggests consumer spending may actually be recovering a bit. The severe equity market volatility in early Q2 likely caused some disruptions in consumer activity, which is now recovering. Again, growth is not particularly fast, but signs do not point to recession—they point to modest ongoing economic growth.

Loan receivables fell 2% to $100 billion. Since last April, the company has run with more restrictive underwriting standards, and this has limited credit creation, pressuring balances. While Sychrony is retaining the majority of this tightening, it is normalizing lending standards a bit, which should help facilitate some balance growth, especially as its Wal-Mart partnership ramps up. Synchrony’s partnership with Wal-Mart has just launched, which should help to drive additional volumes and balances.

Interestingly, consumers are paying down balances more quickly with its payment rate up 60bps to 16.3%. This speaks to consumers that are not struggling to manage their bills, actually prepaying more debt more quickly. While this is a positive for credit quality, it is a negative for loan balances and interest income. In fact, due to higher payment activity, SYF trimmed its revenue outlook to ~$15.05 billion, down $100 million.

In Q3, Synchrony’s net interest margin (“NIM”) expanded by 58bps to 15.62%. In general, I view Fed rate cuts as a positive for SYF’s NIM. Credit card interest rates are only loosely tied to the Fed funds rate whereas its deposit and floating-rate liabilities quickly reset lower. As such, I expect some incremental NIM expansion next year, assuming 3-4 rate cuts over the next year.

Looking at credit quality, delinquencies were also down 39bps from last year at 4.39% while charge-offs fell 90bps to 5.16%. Loss rates are well within its 5.5-6.0% medium-term target, pointing to a targeted credit performance.

October update was encouraging

This past week the company released updated credit metrics for the month of October, which showed a similar picture. Loans grew by $200 million to $100.4 billion. 30+ day delinquencies did tick up to 4.5%, though it is not unusual to see a seasonal tick-up in H2 of the year. Delinquencies are still 40bps better than a year ago. Charge-offs of 5.3% were in-line with the Q2 average and better than the 6.4% reported last year. This October data is consistent with a consumer credit situation that has pockets of weakness but is essentially stable.

Speaking at a conference after releasing the October results, management said it is “pleased” with credit quality and does not see any alarming data inside of delinquencies that point to worsening metrics down the road. Frankly, Synchrony’s experience is largely consistent with my view that we have been experiencing credit normalization more so than deterioration, though its underwriting performance has exceeded my expectations.

Macro backdrop is consistent with normalization

As you can see below, credit card balance have risen dramatically since COVID. I would note that there was a $41 billion reclass in this data series in January of this year. Excluding this, credit card balances are up less than 2% from last year, a fairly modest pace of growth, which reflects the fact that consumers have been pulling back on some discretionary spending in this uncertain economic environment.

While the total level of debt has led to some fears about a potential credit crunch, incomes have also risen considerably. Credit card debt to income ratios are actually still a bit below pre-COVID levels, which was a healthy time for consumer credit. This continues to be a major reason why I view a further uptick in defaults and credit costs as unlikely to be material. Most of the increase over the past three years is really a return to more normal levels, rather than a concerning trend. That said, if we were to see employment fall meaningfully, that would be a headwind for consumer credit quality and lead to incremental losses.

Now, provisions for losses in Q3 were down $451 million to $1.1 billion as the company released $152 million of reserves, reflecting the stabilizing delinquency backdrop in the quarter. Even with their decline, allowances cover 235% of delinquencies. This is right in the range of 200-250% coverage that I like to see. Particularly if delinquencies have peaked (apart from seasonal variations), SYF’s reserves are well-positioned. While we may not see meaningful further releases, it will likely grow reserves more slowly than loan balances.

2026 outlook is encouraging

Back in July, I was targeting $7.60-$8.10 in EPS. However, SYF has earned $7.25 already this year as volumes bounce back and credit continues to outperform. I now see the company earning at least $9.25 this year. With credit conditions stable and some modest loan growth, I see SYF poised to earn at least $9 next year, even without the same degree of reserve releases. In fact, I have some optimism about the credit backdrop next year.

Normally, we see delinquencies improve in Q1 and then gradually build during the year. That is because consumers typically use tax refunds received in Q1 to catch up on delinquencies, which then gradually build back up. I am increasingly optimistic that we will see a larger-than-expected improvement in credit quality (and consumer trends in general during Q1). There has rightly been a lot of focus this year on weak consumer sentiment and the rise in inflation, likely due in part to tariffs.

However, the tax bill passed in July did include offsetting tax cuts. These cuts, namely new or enlarged credits and deductions for tips, overtime, and state taxes, do not impact withholding. In other words, 2025 consumer incomes received no benefit. Instead, they will be applied when consumers file their taxes, which should make this refund seasonal unusually large. I expect a $15-30 billion larger tax refund windfall than normal. This will help more consumers catch up on their loans and support incremental spending. In other words, we may see continued favorable momentum in credit trends in early 2026, which does not appear to be factored into outlooks.

Shares are attractive

Synchrony has an excellent balance sheet as well. SYF ended the quarter with a 13.7% common equity tier 1 (CET1) capital ratio, an extremely healthy level. This gives the company significant excess capital to support potential loan growth as well as return capital to shareholders, which the company had been doing aggressively.

Given its capital position, it did $861 million of buybacks in the quarter, and its share count is down over 6.5% from last year. In September, the company authorized $1 billion of additional buyback capacity, raising the authorization to $2.1 billion. I expect at least 5% share count reduction over the next year. Aside from buybacks, shares offer a 1.6% dividend yield. I expect buybacks to remain the primary source of capital returns. These buybacks can also support EPS growth even with some incremental loan loss reserves.

With strong Q3 results, a continuation so far in Q4, and solid outlook for 2026, SYF is poised to maintain $9 of EPS. After a period of consolidation, I see shares positioned to resume their rally and view 9-10x earnings as a fair multiple given the cyclicality of its credit exposure. That suggests an $83-$90 fair value. With over 15% upside, I view shares as attractive and am upgrading SYF to buy as I see credit metrics poised to outperform consensus in H1 2026.

data.title: Don't Buy Brookfield Unless You Understand This
data.description:
Brookfield Corporation (BN) is a much revered Asset Management and Alternative Asset company, helmed by legendary Canadian investor, Bruce Flatt. Long terms performance for investors has been strong, with a 10-year total return of 345%, materially outperforming the S&P500 (SPY).

Brookfield has a strong focus on real assets, via its sponsorship of Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP) and Brookfield Energy Partners (BEP), and a large Commercial Real Estate Portfolio. Its Asset Management arm Brookfield Asset Management (BAM) operates mainly as an alternative asset manager, with a heavy focus on Brookfield and other real assets.

The Brookfield investment case is based around gaining exposure to real assets. The case for real assets was a focus of Bruce Flatt's recent letter to shareholders. Flatt underlined the shift in central bank policies around the world to continue to issue debt rather than restrain spending, while engaging in yield curve control to suppress interest rates. With interest rates below the rate of inflation, the argument is that ownership of real assets will protect wealth. Real assets have the ability too generate inflation linked cash flows, and deliver real inflation linked returns.

Investors in Brookfield generally focus on this asset story, which is a compelling one. However, recently Brookfield has pivoted to expand heavily into the insurance business with the founding of Brookfield Wealth Management (BNT) as a way of generating asset growth.

BNT has grown rapidly, and is now a meaningful part of BN. However, BN is a very different business to the rest of the company, and its activities generate significant liabilities. I believe that investors in BN need to understand the liability side of the BNT balance sheet in detail, as the risk profile of BN as an investment is significantly changed as a result of the development of BNT. As something of an expert in insurance, I have observed that insurance businesses that focus on the asset generation without due care of the liability management can underperform.

This article looks at the BN 3rd quarter results, and the contribution of BNT. Then I'll move onto the BNT 3rd quarter results to comment on the business performance there, with a detailed look at the liability side of the BNT balance sheet.

Brookfield Corporation Q3 Results

Brookfield released Q3 results on 13th November.

Net income attributable to shareholders for the quarter was $219m, up from $64m year on year. This brings the 9 months net income to $996, up from $908 - close to 10% year on year growth.

Distributable earnings for the quarter were up 5.8% which pulled the year to date distributable earnings up less than 1% year on year.

While this was a solid quarter, most of the heavy lifting on distributable earnings for the quarter was done by BNT, which grew distributable earnings by 15% to $420m, which was 31.5% of the total.

Asset Management (BAM) distributable earnings for the quarter were slightly down, while the operating businesses (BIP) (BEP) etc grew only 2.8%.

So the BNT earnings are starting to be critical to the performance of Brookfield as a whole.

This looks as though this will continue in the long run, as the capital and cash flows are increasingly dependent on BNT.

Brookfield reports under IFRS accounting, the standard in Canada, but uses 'blended' metrics, which are a mix of IFRS accounting and market valuation metrics.

Year on year, blended capital has grown by $14.2bn, an impressive 8.4%, and cashflow by $100m, a more modest 3.5%.

However, on a GAAP basis, the capital is about 1/3 of this. While on a blended basis, overall capital grew by $10bn, and was equally split between the three units, GAAP capital grew by only $1.8bn m. Asset management capital shrank by $400m, and the operating business grew by $1.1bn. The bulk of the capital generation of $1.66bn was generated by BNT.

Net of increased debt, capital per share increased by just 2.3% on a GAAP basis.

So if the growth in market prices of the listed businesses is discarded, the real capital generation is heavily skewed toward BNT.

So overall, Brookfield is shifting from a real asset company into a blended real asset and financial asset company, with the financial assets primarily sitting on the insurance balance sheet.

This has significant implications for the asset mix, the valuation, and the risks facing the company.

Investors who own Brookfield should understand what they are buying with BNT.

Brookfield Wealth Solutions (BNT)

BNT reports in the same cycle as BN, and published their 6-k for the third quarter on Nov 14th.

This followed the results released the prior day.

BNT is, as discussed, an insurance company operating in Property Casualty, Life and Annuities and Run-off solutions. BNT is domiciled in Bermuda, which has a low tax regime and is a popular domicile for insurance companies. I have been covering BNT since this introductory article in 2023, which details the business profile.

Important for investors is that shares in BNT are exchangeable 1-1 for BN shares. For this reason, investors see BNT as a tax friendly tracker stock for BN. I argue that this is too simplistic a view.

The Business Profile

We've discussed the overall business growth as shown below. The quarter on quarter net income was up 10-fold.

Net premiums for the ytd of just under $4.7bn were split pretty evenly between Property-Casualty and Annuity-Life.

The big profit driver was from the investment returns on the annuity business.

The Asset Portfolio

Of the $110 bn in assets in the BNT balance sheet, 56% is fixed income securities, 10% is mortgage loans (RMBS), 7.5% private credit and only 7% is real estate, which is the only non financial asset in the asset mix.

This highlights the importance of financial assets to the overall company performance. The ytd investment income of $4.4bn was dominated by fixed income followed by investment funds (with an undisclosed asset base) and mortgage loans and private credit.

This basically highlights the asset base which investors in BNT are buying - it's basically a fixed income portfolio.

So basically, an investment in BNT is an investment in a fixed income asset portfolio, not a real asset portfolio.

As BNT grows larger, this shifts the asset mix of an investment in Brookfield Corporation (BN).

The Liability Portfolio

The asset gathering has been funded through the assumption of insurance liabilities. This is very different from funding assets from investors - as it comes with complex insurance liabilities. A successful insurance company has to be as successful in underwriting as it is in investment.

Annuity And Life

A large part of the balance sheet is in the sale of annuities, with a smaller life insurance business.

With annuities, the policyholder deposits a lump sum with the insurer, in return for a commitment for the company to make regular payments to the policyholder over a number of years, or for the lifetime of the policyholder. Payments can be fixed, or variable depending on investment performance.

The life insurance contracts are usually simple death based policy payments.

Much of the annuity business is non risk investment stewardship, however there is longevity risk and benefit risk, which varies depending on the payment guarantees baked into the annuity agreements.

The longevity risk with whole of life annuities is that the policy holder outlives the actuarial expectations in the policy pricing. The benefit risk is the investment returns are not sufficient to fund the guaranteed annuity benefits.

Conversely, the life business takes on mortality risk, if benefits are paid out sooner than expected if a policyholder dies. To a degree the life business and annuity business hedge each other, but this is an imperfect hedge, as the buyers of life and annuity products are not necessarily in the same demographic.

Another big risk is lapse risk. Life and annuity insurers pay large commissions to agents and brokers, and these costs are paid up front. If policies are cashed in early (surrendered or lapsed) the insurers have to pay out the surrender value, but they can't claw back the commissions paid.

You can see that the surrender rate is quite high at about 10% of the total in force business, or $8bn for the year to date. This compares to just under $13bn of new premiums - quite a large potential profit drag.

The P&C Portfolio

The major P&C business is Argo, which was acquired in late 2023. Argo is a specialty insurer with a poor past track record of profitability. it operates primarily and casualty and specific specialty P&C lines of business. These are generally longer tail lines of business, with a delay of several years between collecting the premiums and paying out the claims. This is great from a 'float' generation point of view, but assessing longer tail lines requires skillful underwriting.

It is also important to have adequate reserves set aside for future claims. Unfortunately, the casualty market has been generally under-reserving claims for casualty lines, and there are signs of aggressive reserving, as discussed in this report. This helps boost current year results at the expense of future results.

Brookfield acquired Argo for around book value. However, the calculation of book value is based on the in force policy reserves. I have raised doubts whether Argo, which was not a profitable underwriter historically was setting adequate reserves.

The management discussion of the q3 results included some signs of unfavourable development of reserves for prior years. While the numbers are manageable, with around $8bn of total P&C reserves, any material adverse development could significantly impact results.

The estimates for ultimate incurred claims attributable to insured events of prior years increased by $38 million and increased by $125 million, respectively, for the nine months ended September 30, 2025 and 2024. The unfavorable development during the period in 2025 was primarily related to higher-than-anticipated losses within certain casualty lines, which were partially offset by favorable development in our specialty lines. The unfavorable development during the period in 2024 was primarily related to higher-than-anticipated losses within certain run-off lines.

The key to monitor this is to examine the actuarial reserve triangles. These are only presented in the full year results. I'll be diving into those in due course.

Summary

Brookfield Corporation (BN) is a very popular stock with a great track record. Investors are attracted by its exposure to real assets, and view a BN shareholding as a 'real asset' portfolio allocation.

BN has made a strategic pivot into insurance with Brookfield Wealth Solutions (BNT), which writes mainly annuities and property-casualty insurance. This has so been successful as a new driver of growth that BNT is starting to drive the growth profile for BN.

The weighting of the BN business is now becoming skewed towards financial, rather than real assets as a result.

The main asset owned by BNT is fixed income, which carries significant interest rate risk, and market correlation. This in my view weakens the case for holding BN as a real inflation hedge.

In addition the insurance liabilities assumed by BNT carry unique and complex risks which investors need to understand.

I am not saying that this makes Brookfield a poor investment, but that it is a very different one from the Brookfield of the past.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: I Found 2 Deep Value Picks: Buy These Bargains Before Dividends Soar
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

I have a friend who can't drive past an antique store or garage sale. If he sees a sign, he's pulling over to take a look. Once, we were trying to make a two-hour drive, and it took a good four hours with all the stops.

He goes through all the stuff that, in my mind, is old "junk," and he would find value. In absolute terms, it does have value, and he has a nice little side business of identifying items that are mispriced and reselling them to someone who values them. He is an amateur "American Picker."

Now I can't tell you the difference between an old broken watch that's in fact an old broken watch, and one that's something special that a collector will pay big dollars for. Though I have a friend I can call to tell me!

I do my value digging in the stock market. And sometimes, there are picks that have a little rust on them, might have a few dents, but they have features that will likely make them much more valuable in the future.

Today, I want to take a look at two companies that found themselves negatively impacted by rising interest rates. Prices declined, dividends declined, but they're in the process of rebuilding their businesses. As they do, we expect prices and dividends to rise. This makes them deep value picks that are currently being sold at bargain prices.

Let's dive in!

Pick #1: ACRE – Yield 12.3%

Ares Commercial Real Estate (ACRE) is a commercial mortgage REIT. It originates and holds mortgages that it originates itself. These are typically floating-rate mortgages with terms that average three years.

ACRE had a quiet quarter, and that's great news in the market's eyes. ACRE reported DE (distributable earnings) of $0.13, excluding $0.03 in realized losses. Book value came in at $9.47, down from $9.52 in Q2. Finally, ACRE's watchlist remained unchanged.

In terms of the dividend, we should see the chorus of "the dividend is going to get cut again" rhetoric die down. While ACRE fell short this quarter, the path to covering the dividend is very straightforward. In Q3, ACRE received $162 million in repayments and closed only $93 million in loans. As we've said before, a smaller portfolio generates lower earnings. As a result of a smaller portfolio, ACRE's leverage ticked down to 1.1x from 1.2x. ACRE has a lot of room to leverage up.

Most importantly, ACRE announced that subsequent to quarter end, it closed on $271 million in loans. These are early enough in the quarter to be meaningful for DE in Q4. We expect that this is enough for ACRE to cover the distribution in Q4.

Note that ACRE's $0.60 dividend is only 6% of its $9.47 book value. That's very low for this business, which should be able to produce a 10%-15% yield on book value. How does ACRE get back to producing Distributable Earnings of $0.97-$1.40/year?

The problem lies in the highlighted portion of their portfolio: Source

In particular, the risk 4 and 5 rated loans are producing no cash flow at all. The REO (real-estate owned) is producing cash flow, but at a lower ROE. The $381 million in loans that aren't performing is capital that could be producing a lot of cash flow, but isn't right now.

The goal for ACRE is to extract that cash, balancing and taking a loss against the value of putting cash to work sooner. ACRE management says that they're making progress, but real estate is not a fast-moving industry. Deals take months to negotiate, do the due diligence, and finalize.

ACRE's recovery to more normal operating returns is dependent upon working these situations out. The $9.47 in book value includes CECL (current-expected credit loss), so that builds in the realization of some loss on these properties. Actual losses could be higher or lower.

In the meantime, due to ACRE trading at a steep discount to book value, we can collect a double-digit yield on our capital with new investments today, even though the company itself is paying out a conservative 6%. Over the next year, we expect them to extract capital from some of their higher-risk properties and put it back to work. It will probably be 2-3 years before they are back to running at full speed, but we expect some distribution increase to occur mid to late next year.

Pick #2: AQN – Yield 4.2%

Algonquin Power & Utilities Corp (AQN) is a utility company that's in the process of refocusing on regulated utilities after the failed attempt to acquire Kentucky Power. The attempt ended up being extremely expensive, as AQN took out significant debt to prove they could pay for the utility, until the combination of regulatory hurdles and rising interest rates caused the deal to collapse.

With a new CEO at the helm and activist investor Starboard in their corner, AQN laid out the following guidance in June: Source

They guided for $0.30-$0.32 adjusted EPS for 2025, and through Q3, they're at $0.28, turning in $0.09 for the quarter. This was $0.01 higher than Q3 last year despite the sale of Atlantica and loss of that dividend. Source

The main positive drivers were lower interest expense and higher utility rates.

The market seems to like AQN's new direction, with its share price up nearly 40% year-to-date. We're maintaining our position because we believe it has more room to run as management executes its plan.

Conclusion

Many people in the market focus on what's new and trendy right now – which is great, until the trend changes. Today, we looked at two picks that were once flying high, but then economic conditions changed. High interest rates stopped their growth and created challenges in their business models.

Now, economic conditions are becoming more accommodating. At the same time, these businesses are close to resolving the challenges from the past. These are deep value picks that are trading at very large discounts and paying us a dividend while we wait for the next upswing.

We're buying more ACRE and holding AQN because we believe the future value and the future dividends will be higher than they are right now. When it comes to the stock market, don't be afraid to buy low. That often means wading through tickers that others consider to be "junk" and finding the gems of value that the market leaves behind.

data.title: Tempus AI: Opportunity To Build Position
data.description:
Tempus AI, Inc. (TEM) has slumped along with the recent AI pullback, offering another opportunity to build a position in the AI precision medicine stock. The company recently beat Q3 estimates, so the volatile trading is more market-related due to the sudden negative sentiment change on AI stocks, than anything Tempus AI did to harm the business prospects. My investment thesis remains bullish on the stock after the dramatic selloff and dip back to a more attractive valuation.

Merger Boost Ending

Tempus AI bought Ambry at the start of the year to artificially boost the reported growth rates. Unfortunately, the stock market does a horrible job now of reporting pro-forma sales growth or organic growth rates.

The precision AI company reported Q3'25 sales soared 85% to reach $334 million. Tempus AI beat analyst estimates by a solid $5 million, so the company reported a small beat.

The key is that the Ambry Genetics test business contributed $103 million in sales for the quarter with a solid 33% pro-forma sales boost. The oncology tests from the Tempus genomics group contributed $140 million in sales, up 32% YoY.

The whole genomics testing business reported incredible growth, but the reported 117% growth rate was only due to Ambry not being included in the official Tempus AI sales from Q3'24. Besides that, the business remains strong, with the oncology segment continuing to both grow the diagnostic tests delivered and also boost the average revenue per test to $1,600 in Q3, more than double from levels just a few years ago.

Tempus AI is always adding new tests, such as the ECG-Low EF software that utilizes AI to help identify patients who may be experiencing a low left ventricular ejection fraction. Or the Tempus Pixel, an AI-powered cardiac imaging platform, both obtaining FDA clearance during Q3.

The data licensing business added $150 million in contract value during the quarter. Tempus AI signed 4 deals of nearly $10 million in contract value or more, led by a 4-year, $66 million contract for a new biotech customer. The data licensing business has been on a roll this year after the expanded AstraZeneca (AZN) deal worth $200 million over a 3-year period was announced in the prior quarter.

Back To Earth

The stock soared from a low of $40 in early April, when the stock market was weak, to over $104 during 2025, due in part to reported quarterly sales jumping from $200 million for the December quarter to $334 million in the September quarter. The whole AI momentum likely swept up the AI precision medicine stock.

The company continues reporting relatively consistent losses, probably contributing to some of the negativity on the stock. Tempus AI only reported an adjusted EBITDA profit of $1.5 million in Q3, with the non-GAAP loss only improving from $0.24 to $0.11.

Clearly, the stock got far too expensive when running to over $100 and trading at over 10x forward sales targets. Tempus AI has slumped back to below $70, and the stock is more rational now at ~7.5x 2026 sales targets of $1.57 billion.

Another reason for the reset, the stock market is probably catching on to the 2026 sales growth targets in the 25% range. On the Q3 '25 investor letter, the CEO reinforced the difficulty of growing at scale, with 25% revenue growth requiring over $300 million in additional sales next year.

Anyone buying for the reported growth rates during 2025 exceeding 80% likely snapped back to reality that the long-term growth trajectory is more in the 25% range, with the scale reaching $1.25 billion this year.

The biggest risk is a dip back towards the $50s, where Tempus AI would trade more like 6x sales estimates. An investor would obtain a much better entry point, but Tempus AI has the growth rate and the exciting potential around precision medicine to avoid trading this low again.

The company forecast Q4 adjusted EBITDA topping $20 million in Q4 for a slightly positive full-year number. The company bought Paige adding $5 million in quarterly costs, and weak adjusted EBITDA guidance for 2026 could turn off a lot of investors looking for profitable AI stocks going forward.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Tempus AI has returned back to earth after a major irrational rally. The stock is more reasonably priced for a company looking at long-term growth rates around the 25% clip.

Investors should use the weakness to load up on Tempus AI while holding some capital back to buy the stock on further weakness to end the year.

data.title: Nebius: A Steal After AI Meltdown - Rating Upgrade To Buy
data.description:
I previously covered Nebius Group (NBIS) in August 2025, discussing how it continued to deliver outsized performance in the neocloud end market, as they reported adj. EBITDA profitability while raising the FY2025 Annualized Recurring Revenue [ARR] and hinted at further growth opportunities in FY2026.

Despite the promising autonomous/robot delivery segments and the rich balance sheet, I had believed that its rally had also occurred overly fast/furiously, with the expensive valuations and the notably higher short interest volumes triggering my Hold rating then.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am finally upgrading the NBIS stock to a Buy, thanks to the improved margin of safety/cheaper valuation arising from the recent meltdown, the growing cloud backlog, and the robust robotaxi monetization opportunity from the end of FY2025 onwards.

NBIS Successfully Monetizes The AI Boom

NBIS 1Y Stock Price

Since my last Hold rating, NBIS has more than doubled to chart a new height of $140s ranges by early October 2025, before losing all of the September 2025 gains to retest the $80s by the time of writing.

This development is unsurprising indeed, with the stock's previously expensive valuations being a double edged sword while triggering the notably steeper losses against those observed in its hyperscaler/neocloud peers.

Otherwise, I am of the opinion that the correction is mostly sentiment/momentum driven, given NBIS' excellent fundamental performance in FQ3'25, as observed in the accelerating Nebius cloud revenue growth to $131.1M (+21.9% QoQ/+383.7% YoY) and the reiterated FY2025 ARR of $1B at the midpoint (+354.5% from March 2025 levels of $220M).

This is especially since the neocloud has offered a promising FY2026 ARR of $8B at the midpoint (+700% YoY), with it highlighting its high-growth stage significantly aided by the improving operating leverage with "adjusted EBITDA margin for the core infrastructure business expanded quarter-over-quarter to nearly 19%."

Similar to another neocloud, CoreWeave (CRWV), NBIS continues to report sold out capacity and growing backlog/customer deals across Meta (META) at $3B over the next five years and Microsoft (MSFT) at ~$18.4B through 2031, with "almost all of the revenue from these deals [starting] to be realized and ramp up during the course of 2026."

This reason is also why NBIS has drastically raised their FY2025 capex guidance to $5B, up from the original guidance of $1.5B, with it supporting the management's FY2026 connected power capacity guidance of 900 MW at the midpoint, up notably from FY2024 levels of 100 MW and FY2025 levels of 220MW.

While NBIS has yet to offer FY2026 guidance, I am of the opinion that the neocloud may report a robust performance despite the tougher YoY comparison from FY2025 levels.

This is especially since the neocloud has highlighted their ability to grow their contracted power to 2.5 GW by the end of 2026 (the amount of power in its supply), allowing them to cater to their growing backlog and/or serve their customers' immediate compute needs:

We sold out all of our available capacity. We continue to see a consistent trend. Every time we bring capacity online, we sold all of it. With the new generation of NVIDIA Blackwell is coming online, more customers are interested in purchasing capacity in advance and securing it for a longer period of time...

In fact, the demand for this capacity was overwhelming and the size of the contract was limited to the amount of capacity that we had available, which means that if we had more, we could have sold more. (Seeking Alpha)

Risks To NBIS' High-Growth Cadence

The Consensus Forward Estimates

I am of the opinion that NBIS may remain unprofitable on a net income basis, as observed in the adj. net loss margins of -68.7% in FQ3'25 (+18.3 points QoQ/+54.9 YoY) attributed to the accelerating COGS growth by +333% YoY and the higher depreciation/amortization costs by +321% YoY.

The latter headwind has been similarly experienced by numerous neoclouds/hyperscalers, with the rising depreciation costs and the intensified AI chip replacement cycle likely to trigger a rising proportion of D&A costs on their bottom lines along with an impacted free cash flow generation moving forward.

This is on top of NBIS' potentially intensified financing cadence across dilutive capital raises and debt leveraging, as observed in the recent $4.3B raise and the $4.1B of debt on its balance sheet, worsened by the higher YTD SBC expenses by +316.4% YoY.

These reasons are also why the neocloud's share count has been consistently growing to 241.07M (+15.4% YoY), with further equity dilution more likely than not, despite the management's prior guidance of remaining "dilution-sensitive as we seek to finance future growth opportunities."

As a result, while NBIS may continue to report high-growth cadence moving forward, I also urge investors to temper their expectations, since its bottom-line inflection may take longer than expected.

NBIS' Diversified Growth Prospects Imply Reasonable Growth Premiums

NBIS Valuations

On the one hand, despite the -37.1% meltdown from the 52-week heights, NBIS remains extremely expensive at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 40.57x, compared to its 1Y mean of 12.22x and its peers, including:

  • hyperscaler - Amazon (AMZN) at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 3.75x,
  • hyperscaler - Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) at 8.53x,
  • hyperscaler - Microsoft (MSFT) at 11.69x,
  • neocloud - DigitalOcean (DOCN) at 6.47x,
  • neocloud - CRWV at 11.18x,
  • Bitcoin miner turned HPC provider - Core Scientific (CORZ) at 15.44x, and
  • Bitcoin miner turned HPC provider - TeraWulf (WULF) at 30.54x.

On the other hand, assuming that the NBIS is able to deliver the consensus raised FY2027 revenue estimates of $4.34B, the neocloud appears to be cheap at my estimated FY2027 EV/Sales valuations of 5.07x, based on the current enterprise value of $22.04B (compared to NBIS' META/MSFT backlog of ~$21B).

The consensus numbers do not appear overly aggressive as well, as observed in the excellent neocloud prospects discussed above.

Readers must not forget NBIS' robust robotaxi monetization opportunity as well, thanks to the upcoming launch of "Avride’s robotaxi service on the Uber platform, is planned in Dallas by the end of 2025."

This builds upon Avride’s delivery robots, which "are already fulfilling orders through the Uber Eats platform for hundreds of restaurants in Jersey City, Austin, and Dallas" - which may trigger an acceleration in the segment's revenue performance from the $0.2M reported in FQ3'25 (+100% YoY).

This is especially since Avride has received $375M in overall funding from Uber (UBER), allowing the former to "accelerate the growth of its fleet, support AI-driven product development, and expand its offering into new geographies," with FY2026 likely to bring forth Avride's excellent growth numbers.

NBIS Is Upgraded To A Buy

NBIS 1Y Stock Price

For now, NBIS has returned most of its recent gains to retest the prior uptrend support line, albeit with it remaining to be seen if the bulls may defend the Q4'25 support levels of $80s.

NBIS Technical Indicators

Despite NBIS' correction already triggering the oversold RSIs and the moderating volumes, the stock remains highly shorted at 10.3% as well, with further volatility more likely than not.

While the neocloud may be a beneficiary of the ongoing cloud and robotaxi boom, I am of the opinion that the stock may need to further pull back for an improved margin of safety.

This is preferably at the next support levels of $76s, with those levels also unlocking an estimated enterprise value of ~$17B, a cheaper FWD EV/sales valuation of ~31x, and an estimated FY2027 EV/sales valuation of 3.91x (nearer to the sector median of 3.56x).

As a result, my upgraded Buy rating for the NBIS stock comes with the caveat that investors wait for my Buy Zone to materialize for a lower dollar cost average and an improved long-term return profile.

Given the potential -14% downside from current levels, I urge some patience in the meantime.
